text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 17 February 2015

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since her last contact with our service, she reports ongoing difficulties with fatigue and variable alertness across the day. She has been actively reducing social alcohol use over recent months, noting fewer late evenings and better morning energy; this has coincided with a small improvement in daytime concentration, though she still experiences patchy attention in the afternoons. She denies recreational drug use. Sleep is generally 6–7 hours, often fragmented. There is no new history of head injury, illness, or intercurrent infection.

In discussing recent events with the patient and her caregiver, Seizure control is inconsistent and while the caregiver perceives stability, she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year, this has been her enduring seizure profile for many years. The caregiver keeps a diary and feels patterns are steady, whereas the patient highlights disrupted sleep and morning myalgia consistent with nocturnal convulsive activity. No clear precipitant was identified aside from occasional missed evening meals and prior higher alcohol intake, which she is now reducing. She has had no emergency department presentations in the past six months and there have been no reported injuries or tongue bites lately.

Current medicines (as reported and reconciled today): Carbamazepine 400 mg b.d.; Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1250 mg b.d.; Topiramate 125 mg b.d.; Sodium Valproate 500 mg b.d.; Clobazam 10 mg b.d. She reports tolerating the regimen without rash or visual disturbance. Main adverse effects are tiredness and mild word-finding difficulty later in the day. Weight stable. Liver function tests from her GP three months ago were within reference ranges (per patient portal screenshot ï¿½ unable to verify today). She mentioned occasional leg itchiness in the evenings with scratch marks noted previously; there are no current breaks in the skin on examination today. Ã‚Â·

We discussed safety: supervision around baths/showers, awareness of nocturnal events, and ensuring a charged phone nearby overnight. She and her caregiver have an action plan for prolonged events and are comfortable with its use. She is keen to continue reducing alcohol intake socially and will aim to maintain more regular sleep. The caregiver confirms adherence with medication using a weekly pill organiser. 

Given the long-standing pattern and her report of fewer late nights since reducing social alcohol, we agreed to maintain current therapy for now while we gather further objective information. I have arranged a short ambulatory EEG with overnight recording to characterise nocturnal events and a basic blood panel (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) via her GP. We will also provide access to our epilepsy nurse specialist for practical support and to review her seizure diary structure. I did not make any dose changes today â€ pending review of the recordings and bloods. If there is evidence of persistent nocturnal convulsive activity on monitoring, we will consider rationalising her polytherapy at the next visit.

Follow-up: 3 months in clinic, sooner if her caregiver notes escalation or if she has any daytime convulsions. She has the contact number for our nurse line for interim queries.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year

Seizure control is inconsistent and while the caregiver perceives stability, she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year, this has been her enduring seizure profile for many years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly describes ongoing seizures: 'she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year'. 'Nightly' implies approximately once every night, i.e., 1 per day. The intermittent tonic seizures occur two times per year, which is less frequent. Per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple exist, the final label should reflect the nightly generalized convulsions: 1 per day.
seizure_frequency_number: she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year
reference: Seizure control is inconsistent and while the caregiver perceives stability, she continues to have nightly generalised convulsions seizures and intermittent tonic seizures two times per year, this has been her enduring seizure profile for many years
